title: Der treue Johannes
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 6. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The sixth tale in the Grimms’ collection (KHM 6), a solemn story of loyalty tested to the utmost. A dying king binds his servant Faithful John to guard the young prince; the prince glimpses the forbidden portrait of the Princess of the Golden Roof and wins her by a merchant’s ruse, whereupon John overhears three ravens foretell three deadly dangers — a bolting horse, a poisoned bridal shirt, and a swoon at the wedding dance — and averts each, though warned that to explain any of it will turn him to stone. Accused of treachery, he speaks and petrifies, and the king can revive him only by slaughtering his own two children, who are then made whole again. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so you will meet older forms such as daß, thun, Thür, todt, gieng and fieng, and the archaic preterite ward.

sentences.t: Es war einmal ein alter König, der war krank und dachte „es wird wohl das Todtenbett sein, auf dem ich liege.“
sentences.l: There was once an old king, who was sick and thought, “it will surely be the deathbed on which I lie.”
sentences.i: Once upon a time there was an old king who was sick, and thought, “This will surely be the deathbed on which I lie.”
sentences.n: “Todtenbett” is the 1857 spelling of Totenbett (deathbed); the old -dt- recurs (todt). “wohl” here softens to ‘surely, no doubt,’ not ‘well.’

sentences.t: Da sprach er „laßt mir den getreuen Johannes kommen.“
sentences.l: Then he spoke, “let faithful John come to me.”
sentences.i: Then he said, “Send faithful John to me.”
sentences.n: “laßt mir … kommen” — the old polite plural imperative (‘have him come to me’); the dative “mir” is a dative of interest. Johannes (John) is left untranslated as a name.

sentences.t: Der getreue Johannes war sein liebster Diener, und hieß so, weil er ihm sein Lebelang so treu gewesen war.
sentences.l: Faithful John was his dearest servant, and was called so because he had been so true to him his whole life long.
sentences.n: “sein Lebelang” = ‘all his life long’ (Lebe- + -lang). “hieß so” — heißen here ‘to be called, named.’

sentences.t: Als er nun vor das Bett kam, sprach der König zu ihm „getreuester Johannes, ich fühle daß mein Ende heran naht, und da habe ich keine andere Sorge als um meinen Sohn: er ist noch in jungen Jahren, wo er sich nicht immer zu rathen weiß, und wenn du mir nicht versprichst ihn zu unterrichten in allem, was er wissen muß, und sein Pflegevater zu sein, so kann ich meine Augen nicht in Ruhe schließen.“
sentences.l: When he now came before the bed, the king spoke to him, “most faithful John, I feel that my end draws near, and I have no other care than for my son: he is still in his young years, when he does not always know how to counsel himself, and if you do not promise me to instruct him in all that he must know, and to be his foster-father, then I cannot close my eyes in peace.”
sentences.i: When he came up to the bed, the king said to him, “Most faithful John, I feel that my end is drawing near, and I have no care but for my son: he is still young, and does not always know how to help himself; and unless you promise me to teach him all he must know, and to be his foster-father, I cannot close my eyes in peace.”
sentences.n: “sich zu rathen wissen” = to know how to help oneself (rathen is the 1857 spelling of raten). “die Augen schließen” (to close one’s eyes) is a gentle idiom for dying in peace. The superlative address “getreuester Johannes” recurs as the king’s tender name for him.

sentences.t: Da antwortete der getreue Johannes „ich will ihn nicht verlassen, und will ihm mit Treue dienen, wenns auch mein Leben kostet.“
sentences.l: Then faithful John answered, “I will not forsake him, and will serve him with faithfulness, even if it costs my life.”
sentences.i: Then faithful John answered, “I will not forsake him, and will serve him faithfully, even if it should cost me my life.”
sentences.n: “wenns” = wenn es, contracted. “dienen” takes the dative (“ihm dienen,” to serve him).

sentences.t: Da sagte der alte König „so sterb ich getrost und in Frieden.“
sentences.l: Then the old king said, “so I die comforted and in peace.”
sentences.i: Then the old king said, “Then I die comforted and at peace.”
sentences.n: “sterb” is the apocopated sterbe (‘I die’) — Grimm often drops the final -e of the first person. “getrost” = reassured, with an untroubled mind.

sentences.t: Und sprach dann weiter „nach meinem Tode sollst du ihm das ganze Schloß zeigen, alle Kammern, Säle und Gewölbe, und alle Schätze, die darin liegen: aber die letzte Kammer in dem langen Gange sollst du ihm nicht zeigen, worin das Bild der Königstochter vom goldenen Dache verborgen steht.
sentences.l: And then spoke further, “after my death you shall show him the whole castle, all the chambers, halls and vaults, and all the treasures that lie therein: but the last chamber in the long passage you shall not show him, wherein the picture of the King’s Daughter of the Golden Roof stands hidden.
sentences.i: And then he went on, “After my death you shall show him the whole castle — all the chambers, halls and vaults, and all the treasures that lie within; but the last chamber in the long passage you shall not show him, where the portrait of the Princess of the Golden Roof stands hidden.
sentences.n: “Gange” keeps the old dative -e (in dem langen Gange). “verborgen steht” — verbergen (to hide) in the past participle plus stehen, ‘stands hidden.’ This forbidden portrait sets the whole tale in motion.

sentences.t: Wenn er das Bild erblickt, wird er eine heftige Liebe zu ihr empfinden, und wird in Ohnmacht niederfallen und wird ihretwegen in große Gefahren gerathen; davor sollst du ihn hüten.“
sentences.l: When he catches sight of the picture, he will feel a violent love for her, and will fall down in a faint, and will fall into great dangers for her sake; from that you shall guard him.”
sentences.i: When he sees the portrait, he will feel a violent love for her, and will fall down in a swoon, and for her sake will come into great dangers; from that you must guard him.”
sentences.n: “in Ohnmacht niederfallen” = to fall down in a faint (Ohnmacht, a swoon). “ihretwegen” = for her sake. “gerathen” is the 1857 spelling of geraten (to fall into a state).

sentences.t: Und als der treue Johannes nochmals dem alten König die Hand darauf gegeben hatte, ward dieser still, legte sein Haupt auf das Kissen und starb.
sentences.l: And when faithful John had once more given the old king his hand on it, this one grew still, laid his head on the pillow and died.
sentences.i: And when faithful John had once more given the old king his hand on it, the old king grew still, laid his head on the pillow, and died.
sentences.n: “die Hand darauf geben” = to give one’s hand on it, i.e. to pledge, seal a promise. “ward” is the archaic preterite of werden (‘became, grew’); modern German uses wurde — you will meet ward throughout. “Haupt” is an elevated word for head.

sentences.t: Als der alte König zu Grabe getragen war, da erzählte der treue Johannes dem jungen König was er seinem Vater auf dem Sterbelager versprochen hatte, und sagte „das will ich gewißlich halten, und will dir treu sein, wie ich ihm gewesen bin, und sollte es mein Leben kosten.“
sentences.l: When the old king had been carried to the grave, faithful John told the young king what he had promised his father on the deathbed, and said, “that I will assuredly keep, and I will be true to you, as I have been to him, even should it cost my life.”
sentences.i: When the old king had been carried to his grave, faithful John told the young king what he had promised his father on his deathbed, and said, “That I will surely keep, and I will be true to you as I was to him, even if it should cost my life.”
sentences.n: “zu Grabe tragen” = to carry to the grave, bury. “Sterbelager” (Sterben + Lager) = deathbed. “gewißlich” is the 1857 spelling of gewisslich (assuredly).

sentences.t: Die Trauer gieng vorüber, da sprach der treue Johannes zu ihm „es ist nun Zeit, daß du dein Erbe siehst: ich will dir dein väterliches Schloß zeigen.“
sentences.l: The mourning passed by; then faithful John spoke to him, “it is now time that you see your inheritance: I will show you your paternal castle.”
sentences.i: The mourning passed; then faithful John said to him, “It is now time that you saw your inheritance: I will show you your father’s castle.”
sentences.n: “gieng … vorüber” (vorübergehen, to pass by) is separable; gieng is the 1857 spelling of ging. “väterliches Schloß” = paternal / one’s father’s castle.

sentences.t: Da führte er ihn überall herum, auf und ab, und ließ ihn alle die Reichthümer und prächtigen Kammern sehen: nur die eine Kammer öffnete er nicht, worin das gefährliche Bild stand.
sentences.l: Then he led him everywhere about, up and down, and let him see all the riches and splendid chambers: only the one chamber he did not open, wherein the dangerous picture stood.
sentences.i: So he led him about everywhere, up and down, and let him see all the riches and splendid chambers; only the one chamber he did not open, in which the dangerous portrait stood.
sentences.n: “ließ ihn … sehen” = let him see (lassen + infinitive). The single unopened door is the tale’s tension, as with the forbidden room in other Grimm stories.

sentences.t: Das Bild war aber so gestellt, daß, wenn die Thüre aufgieng, man gerade darauf sah, und war so herrlich gemacht, daß man meinte es leibte und lebte, und es gäbe nichts lieblicheres und schöneres auf der ganzen Welt.
sentences.l: But the picture was so placed that, when the door went open, one looked straight upon it, and was made so gloriously that one thought it lived and breathed, and there were nothing lovelier and more beautiful in all the world.
sentences.i: But the portrait was so placed that whenever the door opened, one looked straight at it, and it was made so gloriously that one thought it lived and breathed, and that there was nothing lovelier or more beautiful in all the world.
sentences.n: “Thüre” is the 1857 spelling of Tür (door), with the old -h-; likewise aufgieng (aufgehen, to open, separable). “es leibte und lebte” is a set phrase, ‘it lived and breathed’ (leiben = to have a body). “gäbe” is subjunctive II (‘there were / would be’), marking the impression rather than plain fact.

sentences.t: Der junge König aber merkte wohl daß der getreue Johannes immer an einer Thür vorübergieng und sprach „warum schließest du mir diese niemals auf?“
sentences.l: But the young king noticed well that faithful John always went past one door and said, “why do you never unlock this one for me?”
sentences.i: But the young king noticed well that faithful John always passed by one door, and said, “Why do you never unlock this one for me?”
sentences.n: “schließest … auf” (aufschließen, to unlock) is separable; the “mir” is a dative of interest (‘for me’). “niemals” = never.

sentences.t: „Es ist etwas darin,“ antwortete er, „vor dem du erschrickst.“
sentences.l: “There is something in it,” he answered, “at which you take fright.”
sentences.i: “There is something in it,” he answered, “that would frighten you.”
sentences.n: “erschrickst” = you take fright (erschrecken); “vor dem” = ‘before/at which,’ the object of the fear.

sentences.t: Aber der König antwortete „ich habe das ganze Schloß gesehen, so will ich auch wissen was darin ist,“ gieng und wollte die Thüre mit Gewalt öffnen.
sentences.l: But the king answered, “I have seen the whole castle, so I also want to know what is in it,” went and wanted to open the door by force.
sentences.i: But the king answered, “I have seen the whole castle, so I want to know what is inside this one too,” and went and would have opened the door by force.
sentences.n: “mit Gewalt” = by force. Note “so will ich” — the resumptive so (‘then, so’) picking up the first clause.

sentences.t: Da hielt ihn der getreue Johannes zurück und sagte „ich habe es deinem Vater vor seinem Tode versprochen, daß du nicht sehen sollst was in der Kammer steht: es könnte dir und mir zu großem Unglück ausschlagen.“
sentences.l: Then faithful John held him back and said, “I promised your father before his death that you shall not see what stands in the chamber: it could turn out to great misfortune for you and me.”
sentences.i: Then faithful John held him back and said, “I promised your father before his death that you should not see what is in the chamber; it could end in great misfortune for you and me.”
sentences.n: “hielt … zurück” (zurückhalten, to hold back) is separable. “zu Unglück ausschlagen” = to turn out to (result in) misfortune (ausschlagen, separable).

sentences.t: „Ach nein,“ antwortete der junge König, „wenn ich nicht hineinkomme, so ists es mein sicheres Verderben: ich würde Tag und Nacht keine Ruhe haben, bis ichs mit meinen Augen gesehen hätte.
sentences.l: “Oh no,” answered the young king, “if I do not get in, then it is my certain ruin: I would have no rest day and night until I had seen it with my own eyes.
sentences.i: “Oh no,” answered the young king, “if I cannot get in, it is my certain undoing: I would have no rest day or night until I had seen it with my own eyes.
sentences.n: “hineinkomme” (hineinkommen, to get in) is separable. “Verderben” = ruin, undoing. “würde … hätte” are subjunctive II — the hypothetical ‘I would have no rest … until I had seen.’ “ichs” = ich es, contracted.

sentences.t: Nun gehe ich nicht von der Stelle, bis du aufgeschlossen hast.“
sentences.l: Now I do not go from the spot until you have unlocked.”
sentences.i: Now I will not stir from this spot until you have unlocked it.”
sentences.n: “nicht von der Stelle gehen” = not to budge, not stir from the spot. “aufgeschlossen” is the past participle of aufschließen.

sentences.t: Da sah der getreue Johannes daß es nicht mehr zu ändern war, und suchte mit schwerem Herzen und vielem Seufzen aus dem großen Bund den Schlüssel heraus.
sentences.l: Then faithful John saw that it was no longer to be changed, and sought out with a heavy heart and much sighing the key from the great bunch.
sentences.i: Then faithful John saw that there was no changing it, and with a heavy heart and many a sigh he sought out the key from the great bunch.
sentences.n: “nicht mehr zu ändern” = ‘not to be changed any more’ (sein + zu + infinitive of possibility). “Bund” here = a bunch, bundle (of keys). “suchte … heraus” (heraussuchen, to search out) is separable.

sentences.t: Als er die Thüre geöffnet hatte, trat er zuerst hinein und dachte er wolle das Bildnis bedecken daß es der König vor ihm nicht sähe: aber was half das? der König stellte sich auf die Fußspitzen und sah ihm über die Schulter.
sentences.l: When he had opened the door, he stepped in first and thought he would cover the likeness so that the king should not see it before him: but what did that help? the king stood himself on his tiptoes and looked over his shoulder.
sentences.i: When he had opened the door, he stepped in first, thinking to cover the portrait so that the king should not see it before him; but what use was that? The king raised himself on tiptoe and looked over his shoulder.
sentences.n: “wolle … sähe” are subjunctives of reported thought and purpose (‘he would … so that the king should not see’). “Bildnis” = likeness, portrait. “auf die Fußspitzen stellen” = to stand on tiptoe (Fußspitze, tip of the foot).

sentences.t: Und als er das Bildnis der Jungfrau erblickte, das so herrlich war und von Gold und Edelsteinen glänzte, da fiel er ohnmächtig zur Erde nieder.
sentences.l: And when he caught sight of the likeness of the maiden, which was so glorious and gleamed with gold and precious stones, he fell down unconscious to the earth.
sentences.i: And when he caught sight of the maiden’s portrait, which was so glorious and gleamed with gold and precious stones, he fell down in a swoon to the ground.
sentences.n: “fiel … nieder” (niederfallen, to fall down) is separable. “ohnmächtig” = unconscious, in a faint — the very danger the old king foretold.

sentences.t: Der getreue Johannes hob ihn auf, trug ihn in sein Bett und dachte voll Sorgen „das Unglück ist geschehen, Herr Gott, was will daraus werden!“ dann stärkte er ihn mit Wein, bis er wieder zu sich selbst kam.
sentences.l: Faithful John lifted him up, carried him to his bed and thought, full of cares, “the misfortune has happened, Lord God, what will come of it!” then he strengthened him with wine until he came to himself again.
sentences.i: Faithful John lifted him up, carried him to his bed, and thought, full of care, “The mischief is done — Lord God, what will come of it!” Then he revived him with wine until he came to himself again.
sentences.n: “hob … auf” (aufheben, to lift up) is separable. “was will daraus werden” = ‘what will come of it’ (werden aus, to become of). “zu sich selbst kommen” = to come to oneself, revive.

sentences.t: Das erste Wort, das er sprach, war „ach! wer ist das schöne Bild?“
sentences.l: The first word that he spoke was, “oh! who is the beautiful picture?”
sentences.i: The first word he spoke was, “Oh! who is that beautiful portrait?”

sentences.t: „Das ist die Königstochter vom goldenen Dache,“ antwortete der treue Johannes.
sentences.l: “That is the King’s Daughter of the Golden Roof,” answered faithful John.
sentences.i: “That is the Princess of the Golden Roof,” answered faithful John.
sentences.n: “vom goldenen Dache” (Dach with the old dative -e) = ‘of the golden roof,’ her fixed epithet through the tale.

sentences.t: Da sprach der König weiter „meine Liebe zu ihr ist so groß, wenn alle Blätter an den Bäumen Zungen wären, sie könntens nicht aussagen; mein Leben setze ich daran, daß ich sie erlange.
sentences.l: Then the king spoke further, “my love for her is so great, if all the leaves on the trees were tongues, they could not utter it; my life I stake on it, that I win her.
sentences.i: Then the king went on, “My love for her is so great that if all the leaves on the trees were tongues, they could not speak it; I stake my life on winning her.
sentences.n: “wären” is subjunctive II (‘if … were’). “könntens” = könnten es. “aussagen” = to say out fully, utter. “sein Leben daran setzen” = to stake one’s life on it; “erlangen” = to attain, win.

sentences.t: Du bist mein getreuster Johannes, du mußt mir beistehen.“
sentences.l: You are my most faithful John, you must stand by me.”
sentences.i: You are my most faithful John — you must stand by me.”
sentences.n: “getreuster” is the contracted superlative of getreu. “beistehen” (+ dative) = to stand by, assist.

sentences.t: Der treue Diener besann sich lange wie die Sache anzufangen wäre, denn es hielt schwer, nur vor das Angesicht der Königstochter zu kommen.
sentences.l: The faithful servant pondered long how the thing were to be begun, for it held hard even to come before the face of the King’s Daughter.
sentences.i: The faithful servant pondered a long while how the matter might be set about, for it was hard even to come before the Princess’s face.
sentences.n: “sich besinnen” = to reflect, ponder. “anzufangen wäre” — anfangen (to begin, set about) as a subjunctive zu-infinitive (‘how it were to be begun’). “es hielt schwer” = it was difficult (an idiom, halten schwer).

sentences.t: Endlich hatte er ein Mittel ausgedacht und sprach zu dem König „alles, was sie um sich hat, ist von Gold, Tische, Stühle, Schüsseln, Becher, Näpfe und alles Hausgeräth; in deinem Schatze liegen fünf Tonnen Goldes, laß eine von den Goldschmieden des Reichs verarbeiten zu allerhand Gefäßen und Geräthschaften, zu allerhand Vögeln, Gewild und wunderbaren Thieren, das wird ihr gefallen, wir wollen damit hinfahren und unser Glück versuchen.“
sentences.l: At last he had thought out a means and spoke to the king, “all that she has about her is of gold — tables, chairs, dishes, cups, bowls and all the household ware; in your treasury lie five tuns of gold; let one of them be worked up by the goldsmiths of the realm into all manner of vessels and implements, into all manner of birds, game and wonderful beasts — that will please her, and we will travel there with it and try our luck.”
sentences.i: At last he had thought of a way, and said to the king, “Everything she has about her is of gold — tables, chairs, dishes, cups, bowls, and all the household ware. In your treasury lie five tuns of gold; have the realm’s goldsmiths work up one of them into all sorts of vessels and utensils, into all sorts of birds, game, and wonderful beasts — that will please her, and we will sail there with it and try our luck.”
sentences.n: “Hausgeräth” and “Geräthschaften” keep the 1857 -th- (household ware, utensils). “Tonnen Goldes” = tuns (barrels) of gold, with the old genitive Goldes. “verarbeiten zu” = to work up into. “Gewild” is an old collective for game / wild creatures. “das wird ihr gefallen” — gefallen takes the dative (it will please her).

sentences.t: Der König hieß alle Goldschmiede herbei holen, die mußten Tag und Nacht arbeiten, bis endlich die herrlichsten Dinge fertig waren.
sentences.l: The king bade all the goldsmiths be fetched hither; they had to work day and night until at last the most glorious things were finished.
sentences.i: The king ordered all the goldsmiths to be fetched, and they had to work day and night until at last the most glorious things were finished.
sentences.n: “hieß … holen” — heißen here = to bid, order (someone to do). “herbei holen” (herbeiholen, to fetch hither) is separable.

sentences.t: Als alles auf ein Schiff geladen war, zog der getreue Johannes Kaufmannskleider an, und der König mußte ein gleiches thun, um sich ganz unkenntlich zu machen.
sentences.l: When everything was loaded onto a ship, faithful John put on merchant’s clothes, and the king had to do a like thing, in order to make himself quite unrecognizable.
sentences.i: When everything was loaded onto a ship, faithful John put on merchant’s clothes, and the king had to do the same, so as to make himself quite unrecognizable.
sentences.n: “zog … an” (anziehen, to put on) is separable. “ein gleiches thun” = to do likewise (thun is the 1857 spelling of tun). “um … zu” = in order to.

sentences.t: Dann fuhren sie über das Meer, und fuhren so lange, bis sie zu der Stadt kamen, worin die Königstochter vom goldenen Dache wohnte.
sentences.l: Then they sailed over the sea, and sailed so long until they came to the city wherein the King’s Daughter of the Golden Roof dwelt.
sentences.i: Then they sailed over the sea, and sailed on until they came to the city where the Princess of the Golden Roof dwelt.
sentences.n: “Meer” = sea. The repeated “fuhren … fuhren so lange, bis” conveys the long voyage — a common Grimm rhythm.

sentences.t: Der treue Johannes hieß den König auf dem Schiffe zurückbleiben und auf ihn warten.
sentences.l: Faithful John bade the king stay behind on the ship and wait for him.
sentences.i: Faithful John bade the king remain behind on the ship and wait for him.
sentences.n: “Schiffe” has the old dative -e. “zurückbleiben” (to stay behind) is separable; “auf ihn warten” = to wait for him.

sentences.t: „Vielleicht,“ sprach er, „bring ich die Königstochter mit, darum sorgt daß alles in Ordnung ist, laßt die Goldgefäße aufstellen und das ganze Schiff ausschmücken.“
sentences.l: “Perhaps,” he spoke, “I bring the King’s Daughter along; therefore see that everything is in order, let the gold vessels be set up and the whole ship be adorned.”
sentences.i: “Perhaps,” he said, “I shall bring the Princess back with me; so see that all is in order — have the gold vessels set out and the whole ship adorned.”
sentences.n: “bring … mit” (mitbringen, to bring along) is separable. “sorgt … laßt” are polite plural imperatives to the crew. “aufstellen” (set up, display) and “ausschmücken” (adorn) are separable.

sentences.t: Darauf suchte er sich in sein Schürzchen allerlei von den Goldsachen zusammen, stieg ans Land und gieng gerade nach dem königlichen Schloß.
sentences.l: Thereupon he gathered together into his little apron all sorts of the gold things, climbed onto land and went straight to the royal castle.
sentences.i: Then he gathered all sorts of the golden things together into his little apron, went ashore, and went straight to the royal castle.
sentences.n: “suchte … zusammen” (zusammensuchen, to gather together) is separable. “Schürzchen” is the diminutive of Schürze (a little apron). “Goldsachen” = golden things (Gold + Sachen).

sentences.t: Als er in den Schloßhof kam, stand da beim Brunnen ein schönes Mädchen, das hatte zwei goldene Eimer in der Hand und schöpfte damit.
sentences.l: When he came into the castle-court, there stood by the well a beautiful girl, who had two golden buckets in her hand and drew water with them.
sentences.i: When he came into the castle courtyard, there by the well stood a beautiful girl, who had two golden pails in her hand and was drawing water with them.
sentences.n: “das hatte” — the demonstrative das resumes the girl (‘she had’); German often points back to a noun this way. “schöpfte” = drew (water), from schöpfen.

sentences.t: Und als es das blinkende Wasser forttragen wollte und sich umdrehte, sah es den fremden Mann und fragte wer er wäre?
sentences.l: And when it wanted to carry away the gleaming water and turned itself round, it saw the strange man and asked who he was?
sentences.i: And as she was about to carry off the gleaming water and turned round, she saw the stranger and asked who he was.
sentences.n: “es” refers to the girl as das Mädchen (neuter), so grammatically ‘it’ — read as ‘she.’ “blinkende” = gleaming (blinken). “wäre” is subjunctive of the reported question (‘who he was’). “forttragen” (to carry away) and “sich umdrehen” (to turn round) are separable.

sentences.t: Da antwortete er „ich bin ein Kaufmann,“ und öffnete sein Schürzchen und ließ sie hineinschauen.
sentences.l: Then he answered, “I am a merchant,” and opened his little apron and let her look in.
sentences.i: Then he answered, “I am a merchant,” and opened his little apron and let her look inside.
sentences.n: “Kaufmann” = merchant — the disguise John and the king have taken. “hineinschauen” (to look in) is separable.

sentences.t: Da rief sie „ei, was für schönes Goldzeug!“ setzte die Eimer nieder und betrachtete eins nach dem andern.
sentences.l: Then she cried, “ei, what beautiful gold-stuff!” set the buckets down and looked at one thing after another.
sentences.i: Then she cried, “Oh, what beautiful golden things!” set down the pails, and examined one piece after another.
sentences.n: “ei” is an exclamation of delighted surprise. “Goldzeug” = golden stuff/wares (Gold + Zeug). “setzte … nieder” (niedersetzen, to set down) is separable; “eins nach dem andern” = one after another.

sentences.t: Da sprach das Mädchen „das muß die Königstochter sehen, die hat so große Freude an den Goldsachen, daß sie euch alles abkauft.“
sentences.l: Then the girl said, “the King’s Daughter must see this; she has such great joy in gold things that she will buy everything from you.”
sentences.i: Then the girl said, “The Princess must see this — she takes such delight in golden things that she will buy everything from you.”
sentences.n: “Freude haben an” (+ dative) = to take delight in. “abkauft” — abkaufen (to buy from someone), separable; the person sold to stands in the dative (euch).

sentences.t: Es nahm ihn bei der Hand und führte ihn hinauf, denn es war die Kammerjungfer.
sentences.l: It took him by the hand and led him up, for it was the chambermaid.
sentences.i: She took him by the hand and led him up, for she was the chambermaid.
sentences.n: Again “es” for das Mädchen (neuter) — read as ‘she.’ “Kammerjungfer” = a lady’s chambermaid.

sentences.t: Als die Königstochter die Waare sah, war sie ganz vergnügt und sprach „es ist so schön gearbeitet, daß ich dir alles abkaufen will.“
sentences.l: When the King’s Daughter saw the wares, she was quite delighted and spoke, “it is so beautifully worked that I will buy everything from you.”
sentences.i: When the Princess saw the wares, she was quite delighted and said, “It is so beautifully wrought that I will buy everything from you.”
sentences.n: “Waare” is the 1857 spelling of Ware (wares, goods). “gearbeitet” = worked, wrought (arbeiten).

sentences.t: Aber der getreue Johannes sprach „ich bin nur der Diener von einem reichen Kaufmann: was ich hier habe ist nichts gegen das, was mein Herr auf seinem Schiff stehen hat, und das ist das künstlichste und köstlichste, was je in Gold ist gearbeitet worden.“
sentences.l: But faithful John spoke, “I am only the servant of a rich merchant: what I have here is nothing against that which my master has standing on his ship, and that is the most artful and most precious that has ever been worked in gold.”
sentences.i: But faithful John said, “I am only the servant of a rich merchant; what I have here is nothing beside what my master has aboard his ship — the most exquisite and precious work that was ever wrought in gold.”
sentences.n: “künstlichste” = most artful, most skilfully wrought (künstlich here in its old sense ‘made with art,’ not ‘artificial’); “köstlichste” = most precious. “je” = ever. The whole line is John’s bait to lure her aboard.

sentences.t: Sie wollte alles herauf gebracht haben, aber er sprach „dazu gehören viele Tage, so groß ist die Menge, und so viel Säle um es aufzustellen, daß euer Haus nicht Raum dafür hat.“
sentences.l: She wanted everything brought up, but he spoke, “to that belong many days, so great is the quantity, and so many halls to set it up, that your house has no room for it.”
sentences.i: She wanted it all brought up, but he said, “That would take many days, the quantity is so great, and so many halls to set it out that your house has no room for it.”
sentences.n: “dazu gehören viele Tage” — gehören here = to be required (‘that takes many days’). “Säle” is the plural of Saal (hall). “aufzustellen” (aufstellen, to set up) is a separable zu-infinitive; “Raum” = room, space.

sentences.t: Da ward ihre Neugierde und Lust immer mehr angeregt, so daß sie endlich sagte „führe mich hin zu dem Schiff, ich will selbst hingehen und deines Herrn Schätze betrachten.“
sentences.l: Then her curiosity and desire were stirred up more and more, so that she at last said, “lead me over to the ship; I will go there myself and behold your master’s treasures.”
sentences.i: Then her curiosity and longing were stirred more and more, so that at last she said, “Lead me over to the ship — I will go there myself and see your master’s treasures.”
sentences.n: “Neugierde” = curiosity; “angeregt” = stirred up, excited (anregen). “führe mich hin” — imperative führe with separable hin (‘lead me over there’). “deines Herrn” = your master’s (genitive).

sentences.t: Da führte sie der getreue Johannes zu dem Schiffe hin und war ganz freudig, und der König, als er sie erblickte, sah daß ihre Schönheit noch größer war, als das Bild sie dargestellt hatte, und meinte nicht anders als das Herz wollte ihm zerspringen.
sentences.l: Then faithful John led her over to the ship and was quite joyful, and the king, when he caught sight of her, saw that her beauty was still greater than the picture had shown her, and thought nothing other than that his heart would burst.
sentences.i: Then faithful John led her over to the ship, full of joy; and the king, when he set eyes on her, saw that her beauty was greater still than the portrait had shown, and thought nothing but that his heart would burst.
sentences.n: “freudig” = joyful. “dargestellt hatte” (darstellen, to depict, separable) — ‘had shown, portrayed.’ “meinte nicht anders als” = ‘thought nothing else but’; “wollte … zerspringen” = was about to burst.

sentences.t: Nun stieg sie in das Schiff, und der König führte sie hinein; der getreue Johannes aber blieb zurück bei dem Steuermann und hieß das Schiff abstoßen, „spannt alle Segel auf, daß es fliegt wie ein Vogel in der Luft.“
sentences.l: Now she stepped into the ship, and the king led her within; but faithful John stayed behind by the helmsman and bade the ship push off, “hoist all sails, that it flies like a bird in the air.”
sentences.i: Now she stepped aboard the ship, and the king led her in; but faithful John stayed behind by the helmsman and bade the ship cast off — “Hoist every sail, that she may fly like a bird through the air.”
sentences.n: “Steuermann” = helmsman. “abstoßen” = to push off, cast off (a boat). “spannt … auf” (aufspannen, to spread, hoist) is a plural imperative to the crew; “Segel” = sail(s).

sentences.t: Der König aber zeigte ihr drinnen das goldene Geschirr, jedes einzeln, die Schüsseln, Becher, Näpfe, die Vögel, das Gewild und die wunderbaren Thiere.
sentences.l: But the king showed her within the golden vessels, each one singly, the dishes, cups, bowls, the birds, the game and the wonderful beasts.
sentences.i: Meanwhile the king showed her, within, the golden ware, each piece one by one — the dishes, cups, bowls, the birds, the game, and the wonderful beasts.
sentences.n: “drinnen” = inside. “jedes einzeln” = each one singly, one by one. “Geschirr” here = tableware, vessels; “Thiere” is the 1857 spelling of Tiere.

sentences.t: Viele Stunden giengen herum, während sie alles besah, und in ihrer Freude merkte sie nicht daß das Schiff dahin fuhr.
sentences.l: Many hours went round while she looked at everything, and in her joy she did not notice that the ship was sailing away.
sentences.i: Many hours went by while she looked at everything, and in her delight she did not notice that the ship was sailing on.
sentences.n: “giengen herum” — herumgehen here of time passing (‘went round, went by’). “besah” (besehen, to look over). “dahin fuhr” = was sailing along/away.

sentences.t: Nachdem sie das letzte betrachtet hatte, dankte sie dem Kaufmann und wollte heim, als sie aber an des Schiffes Rand kam, sah sie daß es fern vom Land auf hohem Meere gieng und mit vollen Segeln forteilte.
sentences.l: After she had looked at the last, she thanked the merchant and wanted home; but when she came to the ship’s edge, she saw that it went far from land on the high sea and sped away under full sails.
sentences.i: After she had looked at the last piece, she thanked the merchant and wished to go home; but when she came to the ship’s rail, she saw that it was far from land on the high sea, speeding away under full sail.
sentences.n: “des Schiffes Rand” = the ship’s edge/rail (genitive). “auf hohem Meere” = on the high sea (Meere, dative). “forteilte” (forteilen, to hurry away) is separable.

sentences.t: „Ach,“ rief sie erschrocken, „ich bin betrogen, ich bin entführt und in die Gewalt eines Kaufmanns gerathen; lieber wollt ich sterben!“
sentences.l: “Oh,” she cried, startled, “I am deceived, I am carried off and fallen into the power of a merchant; rather would I die!”
sentences.i: “Oh,” she cried in dismay, “I am betrayed! I am carried off and fallen into a merchant’s power — I would rather die!”
sentences.n: “entführt” = carried off, abducted (entführen). “in die Gewalt … gerathen” = to fall into the power of (gerathen, 1857 for geraten). “lieber wollt ich sterben” = ‘I would rather die’ (wollt = wollte).

sentences.t: Der König aber faßte sie bei der Hand und sprach „ein Kaufmann bin ich nicht, ich bin ein König und nicht geringer an Geburt als du bist: aber daß ich dich mit List entführt habe, das ist aus übergroßer Liebe geschehen.
sentences.l: But the king took her by the hand and spoke, “a merchant I am not; I am a king, and no lower in birth than you are: but that I have carried you off by cunning, that has happened out of exceedingly great love.
sentences.i: But the king took her by the hand and said, “I am no merchant; I am a king, and of no lower birth than you: but that I carried you off by cunning has come about out of exceedingly great love.
sentences.n: “geringer an Geburt” = lower in birth. “mit List” = by cunning. “übergroßer” = exceedingly great (über- intensifying groß).

sentences.t: Das erstemal, als ich dein Bildnis gesehen habe, bin ich ohnmächtig zur Erde gefallen.“
sentences.l: The first time that I saw your likeness, I fell down unconscious to the earth.”
sentences.i: The first time I saw your portrait, I fell to the ground in a swoon.”
sentences.n: “das erstemal” = the first time. Again “ohnmächtig” (in a faint), echoing the old king’s prophecy.

sentences.t: Als die Königstochter vom goldenen Dache das hörte, ward sie getröstet, und ihr Herz ward ihm geneigt, so daß sie gerne einwilligte seine Gemahlin zu werden.
sentences.l: When the King’s Daughter of the Golden Roof heard that, she was comforted, and her heart became inclined to him, so that she gladly consented to become his consort.
sentences.i: When the Princess of the Golden Roof heard that, she was comforted, and her heart inclined to him, so that she gladly consented to become his wife.
sentences.n: “ward … getröstet” = was comforted (trösten). “ihr Herz ward ihm geneigt” = her heart grew inclined toward him (geneigt, well-disposed). “einwilligen” = to consent; “Gemahlin” = wife, consort.

sentences.t: Es trug sich aber zu, während sie auf dem hohen Meere dahin fuhren, daß der getreue Johannes, als er vorn auf dem Schiffe saß und Musik machte, in der Luft drei Raben erblickte, die daher geflogen kamen.
sentences.l: But it came about, while they were sailing along on the high sea, that faithful John, as he sat at the front of the ship and made music, caught sight of three ravens in the air, which came flying up.
sentences.i: Now it came to pass, while they were sailing on over the high sea, that faithful John, sitting at the prow making music, caught sight of three ravens in the air, that came flying up.
sentences.n: “es trug sich zu” (sich zutragen, to come about, separable) is a solemn fairy-tale phrase. “vorn” = at the front, at the prow. “daher geflogen kamen” = came flying along. The three ravens carry the tale’s prophecy.

sentences.t: Da hörte er auf zu spielen und horchte was sie mit einander sprachen, denn er verstand das wohl.
sentences.l: Then he stopped playing and listened to what they spoke with one another, for he understood that well.
sentences.i: Then he stopped playing and listened to what they said to one another, for he understood it well.
sentences.n: “hörte … auf” (aufhören, to stop) is separable. “horchte” = listened intently. That John understands the birds’ speech is the hinge of the plot.

sentences.t: Die eine rief „ei, da führt er die Königstochter vom goldenen Dache heim.“
sentences.l: The one cried, “ei, there he is leading home the King’s Daughter of the Golden Roof.”
sentences.i: The first cried, “Look — there he brings home the Princess of the Golden Roof!”
sentences.n: “führt … heim” (heimführen, to lead / bring home, of a bride) is separable. “die eine … die zweite … die dritte” number the three ravens.

sentences.t: „Ja,“ antwortete die zweite, „er hat sie noch nicht.“
sentences.l: “Yes,” answered the second, “he does not have her yet.”
sentences.i: “Yes,” answered the second, “but he does not have her yet.”

sentences.t: Sprach die dritte „er hat sie doch, sie sitzt bei ihm im Schiffe.“
sentences.l: Spoke the third, “he does have her, though; she sits by him in the ship.”
sentences.i: The third said, “But he does have her — she is sitting beside him in the ship.”
sentences.n: “doch” here contradicts the previous speaker (‘but he does’). Note the verb-first “Sprach die dritte” — a lively narrative inversion.

sentences.t: Da fieng die erste wieder an und rief „was hilft ihm das! wenn sie ans Land kommen, wird ihm ein fuchsrothes Pferd entgegenspringen: da wird er sich aufschwingen wollen, und thut er das, so sprengt es mit ihm fort und in die Luft hinein, daß er nimmer mehr seine Jungfrau wieder sieht.“
sentences.l: Then the first began again and cried, “what does that help him! when they come to land, a fox-red horse will spring toward him: then he will want to swing himself up onto it, and if he does that, it bursts away with him and up into the air, so that he never more sees his maiden again.”
sentences.i: Then the first began again and cried, “What use is that to him! When they come to land, a chestnut horse will come leaping toward him; he will want to spring up onto it, and if he does, it will bolt away with him up into the air, so that he never sees his maiden again.”
sentences.n: “fieng … an” (anfangen, to begin) is separable, in the 1857 spelling. “fuchsrothes” = fox-red, chestnut (fuchsroth). “entgegenspringen” (to leap toward, + dative) and “aufschwingen” (to swing oneself up) are separable. “thut er das” = ‘if he does that’ (verb-first conditional; thut, 1857 for tut). This is the first of the three dangers.

sentences.t: Sprach die zweite „ist gar keine Rettung?“
sentences.l: Spoke the second, “is there no rescue at all?”
sentences.i: The second said, “Is there no rescue at all?”
sentences.n: “gar keine” = none at all. “Rettung” = rescue, deliverance.

sentences.t: „O ja, wenn ein anderer schnell aufsitzt, das Feuergewehr, das in den Halftern stecken muß, heraus nimmt und das Pferd damit todt schießt, so ist der junge König gerettet.
sentences.l: “Oh yes, if another quickly mounts, takes out the firearm that must be lodged in the holsters, and shoots the horse dead with it, then the young king is saved.
sentences.i: “Oh yes — if someone else mounts quickly, takes out the firearm that must be in the holsters, and shoots the horse dead with it, then the young king is saved.
sentences.n: “aufsitzt” (aufsitzen, to mount) is separable. “Feuergewehr” = firearm; “Halftern” = holsters (dative pl.); “stecken” = to be lodged, stuck. “todt schießt” = shoots dead (todt, 1857 for tot). Each rescue names its price in the next line.

sentences.t: Aber wer weiß das! und wers weiß und sagts ihm, der wird zu Stein von den Fußzehen bis zum Knie.“
sentences.l: But who knows that! and whoever knows it and tells it to him, he turns to stone from the toes up to the knee.”
sentences.i: But who knows it! And whoever knows it and tells him, that one turns to stone from the toes up to the knee.”
sentences.n: “wers” = wer es; “sagts” = sagt es. The stone-curse is the tale’s central bargain: to save his master, the rescuer must never explain — on pain of petrifaction. It advances in stages (toes-to-knee, knee-to-heart, crown-to-toe).

sentences.t: Da sprach die zweite „ich weiß noch mehr, wenn das Pferd auch getödtet wird, so behält der junge König doch nicht seine Braut: wenn sie zusammen ins Schloß kommen, so liegt dort ein gemachtes Brauthemd in einer Schüssel, und sieht aus als wärs von Gold und Silber gewebt, ist aber nichts als Schwefel und Pech: wenn ers anthut, verbrennt es ihn bis aufs Mark und Knochen.“
sentences.l: Then the second spoke, “I know still more: even if the horse is killed, the young king yet does not keep his bride: when they come together into the castle, there lies a made bridal shirt in a dish, and it looks as if it were woven of gold and silver, but is nothing but sulphur and pitch: when he puts it on, it burns him to the marrow and bone.”
sentences.i: Then the second said, “I know still more: even if the horse is killed, the young king will still not keep his bride. When they come together into the castle, there will lie a bridal shirt in a dish, looking as though it were woven of gold and silver, but it is nothing but sulphur and pitch; when he puts it on, it will burn him to the very marrow and bone.”
sentences.n: “getödtet” (1857 for getötet) = killed. “gemachtes Brauthemd” = a made-up (prepared, false) bridal shirt. “wärs” = wäre es (subjunctive, ‘as if it were’). “anthut” (anthun/antun, to put on) is separable. This is the second danger; “Schwefel und Pech” = brimstone and pitch.

sentences.t: Sprach die dritte „ist da gar keine Rettung?“
sentences.l: Spoke the third, “is there no rescue at all there?”
sentences.i: The third said, “Is there no rescue for that at all?”

sentences.t: „O ja,“ antwortete die zweite, „wenn einer mit Handschuhen das Hemd packt und wirft es ins Feuer, daß es verbrennt, so ist der junge König gerettet.
sentences.l: “Oh yes,” answered the second, “if someone grips the shirt with gloves and throws it into the fire, so that it burns up, then the young king is saved.
sentences.i: “Oh yes,” answered the second, “if someone seizes the shirt with gloves and throws it into the fire so that it burns up, then the young king is saved.
sentences.n: “Handschuhen” = gloves (dative pl.). “packt” (packen, to grip) and “wirft” (werfen, to throw) — note the danger must be handled without bare skin. “verbrennt” = burns up.

sentences.t: Aber was hilfts! wers weiß und es ihm sagt, der wird halbes Leibes Stein vom Knie bis zum Herzen.“
sentences.l: But what does it help! whoever knows it and tells it to him, he becomes stone in half his body, from the knee up to the heart.”
sentences.i: But what use is it! Whoever knows it and tells him, that one turns to stone in half his body, from the knee up to the heart.”
sentences.n: “hilfts” = hilft es. “halbes Leibes” = ‘in half his body’ (an old adverbial genitive of Leib, body). The curse now reaches from knee to heart — one stage further than before.

sentences.t: Da sprach die dritte „ich weiß noch mehr, wird das Brauthemd auch verbrannt, so hat der junge König seine Braut doch noch nicht: wenn nach der Hochzeit der Tanz anhebt, und die junge Königin tanzt, wird sie plötzlich erbleichen und wie todt hinfallen: und hebt sie nicht einer auf und zieht aus ihrer rechten Brust drei Tropfen Blut und speit sie wieder aus, so stirbt sie.
sentences.l: Then the third spoke, “I know still more: even if the bridal shirt is burned, the young king still does not yet have his bride: when after the wedding the dance strikes up, and the young queen dances, she will suddenly grow pale and fall down as if dead: and unless someone lifts her up and draws three drops of blood from her right breast and spits them out again, she dies.
sentences.i: Then the third said, “I know still more: even if the bridal shirt is burned, the young king will still not have his bride. When the dance strikes up after the wedding, and the young queen dances, she will suddenly turn pale and fall down as if dead; and unless someone lifts her up and draws three drops of blood from her right breast and spits them out again, she will die.
sentences.n: “anhebt” (anheben, to begin, strike up) is separable. “erbleichen” = to grow pale; “wie todt hinfallen” = to fall down as if dead. “hebt … auf” (aufheben), “zieht … aus,” “speit … aus” (speien, to spew/spit) are separable. This is the third and last danger.

sentences.t: Aber verräth das einer, der es weiß, so wird er ganzes Leibes zu Stein vom Wirbel bis zur Fußzehe.“
sentences.l: But if one who knows it discloses that, then he becomes stone in his whole body, from the crown to the toe.”
sentences.i: But if one who knows it should disclose it, he will turn wholly to stone, from the crown of his head to the toe.”
sentences.n: “verräth” (verrathen, 1857 for verrät) = discloses, betrays. “ganzes Leibes” = ‘in his whole body’ (old genitive). “vom Wirbel bis zur Fußzehe” = from the crown of the head to the toe — the final, total petrifaction.

sentences.t: Als die Raben das mit einander gesprochen hatten, flogen sie weiter, und der getreue Johannes hatte alles wohl verstanden, aber von der Zeit an war er still und traurig; denn verschwieg er seinem Herrn, was er gehört hatte, so war dieser unglücklich: entdeckte er es ihm, so mußte er selbst sein Leben hingeben.
sentences.l: When the ravens had spoken that with one another, they flew on, and faithful John had understood everything well, but from that time on he was silent and sad; for if he kept silent to his master about what he had heard, then this one was unhappy: if he disclosed it to him, then he himself had to give up his life.
sentences.i: When the ravens had spoken all this with one another, they flew on, and faithful John had understood it all; but from that time on he was silent and sorrowful, for if he kept from his master what he had heard, his master would come to grief, and if he told him, he himself must give up his life.
sentences.n: “verschwieg” (verschweigen, to keep silent about) and “entdeckte … ihm” (entdecken here = to disclose to) frame John’s dilemma. “von der Zeit an” = from that time on. Note the verb-first conditionals “verschwieg er … so …” / “entdeckte er … so …” (‘if he kept silent … then …’).

sentences.t: Endlich aber sprach er bei sich „meinen Herrn will ich retten, und sollt ich selbst darüber zu Grunde gehen.“
sentences.l: But at last he spoke to himself, “my master I will save, even should I myself go to ruin over it.”
sentences.i: But at last he said to himself, “I will save my master, even if I myself perish for it.”
sentences.n: “bei sich sprechen” = to say to oneself. “zu Grunde gehen” = to perish, be ruined. “sollt ich” = ‘should I’ (concessive subjunctive). This resolve seals John’s fate.

sentences.t: Als sie nun ans Land kamen, da geschah es, wie die Rabe vorher gesagt hatte, und es sprengte ein prächtiger fuchsrother Gaul daher.
sentences.l: When they now came to land, it happened as the raven had said before, and a splendid fox-red steed came galloping up.
sentences.i: When they came to land, it happened just as the raven had foretold, and a splendid chestnut steed came galloping up.
sentences.n: “sprengte … daher” — sprengen here of a horse galloping (‘came galloping up’). “Gaul” = horse, steed. The first prophesied danger arrives; note the singular “die Rabe” (the raven).

sentences.t: „Wohlan,“ sprach der König, „der soll mich in mein Schloß tragen,“ und wollte sich aufsetzen, doch der treue Johannes kam ihm zuvor, schwang sich schnell darauf, zog das Gewehr aus den Halftern, und schoß den Gaul nieder.
sentences.l: “Well then,” spoke the king, “that one shall carry me into my castle,” and wanted to seat himself on it, but faithful John came before him, swung himself quickly onto it, drew the gun from the holsters, and shot the steed down.
sentences.i: “Come then,” said the king, “this one shall carry me to my castle,” and would have mounted; but faithful John forestalled him, swung himself quickly onto it, drew the gun from the holsters, and shot the steed dead.
sentences.n: “kam ihm zuvor” — zuvorkommen (+ dative) = to forestall, get ahead of someone. “Gewehr” = gun. “schoß … nieder” (niederschießen, to shoot down) is separable. John does exactly what the raven prescribed.

sentences.t: Da riefen die andern Diener des Königs, die dem treuen Johannes doch nicht gut waren, „wie schändlich, das schöne Thier zu tödten, das den König in sein Schloß tragen sollte!“
sentences.l: Then the king’s other servants, who were not well-disposed to faithful John, cried, “how shameful, to kill the beautiful beast that was to carry the king into his castle!”
sentences.i: Then the king’s other servants, who were no friends of faithful John, cried, “How shameful, to kill the fine beast that was to carry the king to his castle!”
sentences.n: “jemandem gut sein” = to be well-disposed toward someone (here in the negative). “schändlich” = shameful, disgraceful. “tödten” is the 1857 spelling of töten (to kill). The servants’ resentment will feed the false accusation to come.

sentences.t: Aber der König sprach „schweigt und laßt ihn gehen, es ist mein getreuester Johannes, wer weiß wozu das gut ist!“
sentences.l: But the king spoke, “be silent and let him go; it is my most faithful John, who knows what it is good for!”
sentences.i: But the king said, “Be silent and let him be — it is my most faithful John; who knows what good it may be for!”
sentences.n: “schweigt” = be silent (plural imperative). “wozu das gut ist” = ‘what that is good for.’ This refrain of trust returns after each of John’s baffling deeds.

sentences.t: Nun giengen sie ins Schloß, und da stand im Saal eine Schüssel, und das gemachte Brauthemd lag darin und sah aus nicht anders als wäre es von Gold und Silber.
sentences.l: Now they went into the castle, and there in the hall stood a dish, and the made bridal shirt lay in it and looked no other than as if it were of gold and silver.
sentences.i: Now they went into the castle, and there in the hall stood a dish, and the false bridal shirt lay in it, looking for all the world as if it were of gold and silver.
sentences.n: “Saal” = hall. “sah aus nicht anders als wäre es …” = ‘looked no other than as if it were …’ (wäre, subjunctive). The second danger, exactly as foretold.

sentences.t: Der junge König gieng darauf zu und wollte es ergreifen, aber der treue Johannes schob ihn weg, packte es mit Handschuhen an, trug es schnell ins Feuer und ließ es verbrennen.
sentences.l: The young king went toward it and wanted to grasp it, but faithful John shoved him away, gripped it with gloves, carried it quickly into the fire and let it burn.
sentences.i: The young king went up to it and would have seized it, but faithful John pushed him away, took hold of it with gloves, carried it quickly into the fire, and let it burn.
sentences.n: “gieng … zu” (zugehen auf) and “packte … an” (anpacken, to take hold of) are separable. “schob … weg” (wegschieben, to shove away). Again John follows the raven’s prescription — with gloves, into the fire.

sentences.t: Die anderen Diener fiengen wieder an zu murren und sagten „seht, nun verbrennt er gar des Königs Brauthemd.“
sentences.l: The other servants began again to grumble and said, “see, now he even burns the king’s bridal shirt.”
sentences.i: The other servants began to grumble again and said, “See, now he is even burning the king’s bridal shirt.”
sentences.n: “fiengen … an” (anfangen, 1857 spelling) is separable. “murren” = to grumble, mutter. “gar” here = even, actually.

sentences.t: Aber der junge König sprach „wer weiß wozu es gut ist, laßt ihn gehen, es ist mein getreuester Johannes.“
sentences.l: But the young king spoke, “who knows what it is good for; let him go, it is my most faithful John.”
sentences.i: But the young king said, “Who knows what good it may be for; let him be — it is my most faithful John.”
sentences.n: The king repeats his trusting refrain — the tale’s threefold structure, mirroring the three dangers.

sentences.t: Nun ward die Hochzeit gefeiert: der Tanz hub an, und die Braut trat auch hinein, da hatte der treue Johannes Acht und schaute ihr ins Antlitz; auf einmal erbleichte sie und fiel wie todt zur Erde.
sentences.l: Now the wedding was celebrated: the dance struck up, and the bride too stepped in; then faithful John took heed and looked into her countenance; all at once she grew pale and fell as if dead to the earth.
sentences.i: Now the wedding was celebrated: the dance struck up, and the bride too came in; then faithful John kept watch and looked into her face — all at once she turned pale and fell to the ground as if dead.
sentences.n: “hub an” — an older strong preterite of anheben (to begin, strike up), separable. “Acht haben” = to take heed, keep watch. “Antlitz” = countenance, face (elevated). “erbleichte” (erbleichen) = grew pale. The third danger, at the very wedding.

sentences.t: Da sprang er eilends hinzu, hob sie auf und trug sie in eine Kammer, da legte er sie nieder, kniete und sog die drei Blutstropfen aus ihrer rechten Brust und speite sie aus.
sentences.l: Then he sprang up in haste, lifted her up and carried her into a chamber; there he laid her down, knelt, and sucked the three drops of blood from her right breast and spat them out.
sentences.i: Then he sprang up in haste, lifted her, and carried her into a chamber; there he laid her down, knelt, sucked the three drops of blood from her right breast, and spat them out.
sentences.n: “sprang … hinzu” (up to her), “hob … auf,” “legte … nieder” are separable. “eilends” = in haste. “sog … aus” (aussaugen, to suck out; sog is the preterite of saugen) and “speite … aus” (ausspeien, to spit out). John performs the last rescue exactly as prescribed.

sentences.t: Alsbald athmete sie wieder und erholte sich, aber der junge König hatte es mit angesehen, und wußte nicht warum es der getreue Johannes gethan hatte, ward zornig darüber, und rief „werft ihn ins Gefängnis.“
sentences.l: At once she breathed again and recovered herself, but the young king had looked on at it, and did not know why faithful John had done it, grew angry about it, and cried, “throw him into prison.”
sentences.i: At once she breathed again and recovered, but the young king had looked on, and, not knowing why faithful John had done it, grew angry and cried, “Throw him into prison!”
sentences.n: “athmete” (1857 for atmete) = breathed; “gethan” (1857 for getan). “mit ansehen” = to look on at (something). “werft” = throw (plural imperative). Because John may not explain, his rescue looks like an outrage — the trap the ravens described.

sentences.t: Am andern Morgen ward der getreue Johannes verurtheilt und zum Galgen geführt, und als er oben stand und gerichtet werden sollte, sprach er „jeder der sterben soll, darf vor seinem Ende noch einmal reden, soll ich das Recht auch haben?“
sentences.l: On the next morning faithful John was condemned and led to the gallows, and when he stood up on it and was to be executed, he spoke, “everyone who is to die may speak once more before his end; shall I have that right too?”
sentences.i: The next morning faithful John was condemned and led to the gallows, and when he stood up there and was to be put to death, he said, “Everyone who is to die may speak once more before his end; shall I have that right too?”
sentences.n: “ward … verurtheilt” (1857 for verurteilt) = was condemned. “Galgen” = gallows. “gerichtet werden” — richten here = to execute (‘to be put to death’). “das Recht haben” = to have the right.

sentences.t: „Ja,“ antwortete der König, „es soll dir vergönnt sein.“
sentences.l: “Yes,” answered the king, “it shall be granted you.”
sentences.i: “Yes,” answered the king, “it shall be granted you.”
sentences.n: “vergönnt sein” = to be granted, allowed (vergönnen). The king’s grant here unwittingly draws out the fatal explanation.

sentences.t: Da sprach der treue Johannes „Ich bin mit Unrecht verurtheilt und bin dir immer treu gewesen,“ und erzählte wie er auf dem Meer das Gespräch der Raben gehört, und wie er, um seinen Herrn zu retten, das alles hätte thun müssen.
sentences.l: Then faithful John spoke, “I am condemned with injustice, and I have always been true to you,” and told how he had heard the ravens’ conversation on the sea, and how, in order to save his master, he had had to do all that.
sentences.i: Then faithful John said, “I am condemned unjustly, and I have always been true to you,” and told how he had heard the ravens’ talk upon the sea, and how, to save his master, he had had to do all that.
sentences.n: “mit Unrecht” = wrongfully. “hätte thun müssen” = ‘had had to do’ (subjunctive of the reported account; thun, 1857 for tun). By explaining, John triggers the stone-curse the ravens foretold.

sentences.t: Da rief der König „o mein treuester Johannes, Gnade! Gnade! führt ihn herunter.“
sentences.l: Then the king cried, “oh my most faithful John, mercy! mercy! lead him down.”
sentences.i: Then the king cried, “Oh, my most faithful John — mercy, mercy! Bring him down!”
sentences.n: “führt … herunter” (herunterführen, to lead down) is a plural imperative — but the reprieve comes too late.

sentences.t: Aber der treue Johannes war bei dem letzten Wort das er geredet hatte leblos herabgefallen, und war ein Stein.
sentences.l: But faithful John, at the last word that he had spoken, had fallen down lifeless, and was a stone.
sentences.i: But faithful John, at the last word he had spoken, had fallen down lifeless, and was turned to stone.
sentences.n: “leblos” = lifeless; “herabgefallen” (herabfallen, to fall down). The prophecy is fulfilled to the letter: he turns wholly to stone. This stone-transformation motif — a curse for a spoken secret, undone only at terrible cost — drives the tale’s end.

sentences.t: Darüber trug nun der König und die Königin großes Leid, und der König sprach „ach, was hab ich große Treue so übel belohnt!“ und ließ das steinerne Bild aufheben und in seine Schlafkammer neben sein Bett stellen.
sentences.l: Over this the king and the queen now bore great grief, and the king spoke, “oh, how ill have I rewarded great faithfulness!” and had the stone image lifted up and set in his sleeping-chamber beside his bed.
sentences.i: At this the king and queen bore great grief, and the king said, “Oh, how ill have I repaid great faithfulness!” and had the stone image raised up and set in his bedchamber beside his bed.
sentences.n: “Leid tragen” = to bear grief. “übel belohnen” = to reward badly, repay ill (übel, ill; belohnen, to reward). “steinerne” = of stone (steinern). “ließ … aufheben … stellen” = had it lifted and set (lassen + infinitive).

sentences.t: So oft er es ansah, weinte er und sprach „ach, könnt ich dich wieder lebendig machen, mein getreuester Johannes.“
sentences.l: As often as he looked at it, he wept and spoke, “oh, could I make you alive again, my most faithful John.”
sentences.i: As often as he looked at it, he wept and said, “Oh, could I but make you alive again, my most faithful John!”
sentences.n: “so oft” = as often as, whenever. “könnt ich” = ‘could I’ (subjunctive of a wish). “lebendig machen” = to bring to life, restore to life.

sentences.t: Es gieng eine Zeit herum, da gebar die Königin Zwillinge, zwei Söhnlein, die wuchsen heran und waren ihre Freude.
sentences.l: A time went round; then the queen bore twins, two little sons, who grew up and were her joy.
sentences.i: Some time went by; then the queen bore twins, two little sons, who grew up and were her joy.
sentences.n: “es gieng eine Zeit herum” = ‘a time went by.’ “Zwillinge” = twins. “Söhnlein” is the diminutive of Sohn (little son). “wuchsen heran” (heranwachsen, to grow up) is separable.

sentences.t: Einmal, als die Königin in der Kirche war, und die zwei Kinder bei dem Vater saßen und spielten, sah dieser wieder das steinerne Bildnis voll Trauer an, seufzte und rief „ach, könnt ich dich wieder lebendig machen, mein getreuester Johannes.“
sentences.l: Once, when the queen was at church and the two children sat by their father and played, he again looked at the stone likeness full of grief, sighed and cried, “oh, could I make you alive again, my most faithful John.”
sentences.i: Once, when the queen was at church and the two children sat by their father and played, he again looked at the stone image full of grief, sighed, and cried, “Oh, could I but make you alive again, my most faithful John!”
sentences.n: “sah … an” (ansehen, to look at) is separable. “voll Trauer” = full of grief. “seufzte” = sighed. The king’s repeated lament finally draws an answer from the stone.

sentences.t: Da fieng der Stein an zu reden und sprach „ja, du kannst mich wieder lebendig machen, wenn du dein Liebstes daran wenden willst.“
sentences.l: Then the stone began to speak and said, “yes, you can make me alive again, if you will spend on it your dearest.”
sentences.i: Then the stone began to speak and said, “Yes, you can make me alive again, if you will give up for it what you hold dearest.”
sentences.n: “fieng … an” (anfangen) is separable. “dein Liebstes daran wenden” — wenden here = to expend, stake (‘to spend on it what you love most’). The dreadful condition is set.

sentences.t: Da rief der König „alles, was ich auf der Welt habe, will ich für dich hingeben.“
sentences.l: Then the king cried, “everything that I have in the world I will give up for you.”
sentences.i: Then the king cried, “Everything that I have in the world I will give up for you.”
sentences.n: “hingeben” (to give up, give away, sacrifice) is separable — spoken before he knows the price.

sentences.t: Sprach der Stein weiter „wenn du mit deiner eigenen Hand deinen beiden Kindern den Kopf abhaust und mich mit ihrem Blute bestreichst, so erhalte ich das Leben wieder.“
sentences.l: The stone spoke further, “if you strike off with your own hand your two children’s heads and smear me with their blood, then I receive life again.”
sentences.i: The stone went on, “If with your own hand you strike off your two children’s heads and smear me with their blood, then I shall receive my life again.”
sentences.n: “den Kopf abhaust” (abhauen, to strike/chop off) is separable; “den Kindern den Kopf abhauen” uses the dative-of-possession style (‘strike off the head to the children,’ i.e. their heads). “bestreichst” (bestreichen, to smear, coat). “erhalte” = receive (erhalten). Grimm does not soften the horror of the price.

sentences.t: Der König erschrack, als er hörte daß er seine liebsten Kinder selbst tödten sollte, doch dachte er an die große Treue, und daß der getreue Johannes für ihn gestorben war, zog sein Schwert und hieb mit eigener Hand den Kindern den Kopf ab.
sentences.l: The king took fright when he heard that he was to kill his dearest children himself, yet he thought of the great faithfulness, and that faithful John had died for him, drew his sword and struck off with his own hand the children’s heads.
sentences.i: The king was aghast when he heard that he was to kill his dearest children himself; yet he thought of that great faithfulness, and that faithful John had died for him, drew his sword, and with his own hand struck off the children’s heads.
sentences.n: “erschrack” is the 1857/archaic preterite of erschrecken (took fright); modern erschrak. “tödten” (1857 for töten). “hieb … ab” (abhauen, to strike off; hieb is the strong preterite of hauen) is separable. The slaughter is carried out plainly, as the tale demands.

sentences.t: Und als er mit ihrem Blute den Stein bestrichen hatte, so kehrte das Leben zurück, und der getreue Johannes stand wieder frisch und gesund vor ihm.
sentences.l: And when he had smeared the stone with their blood, life returned, and faithful John stood before him again fresh and sound.
sentences.i: And when he had smeared the stone with their blood, life returned, and faithful John stood before him again, hale and sound.
sentences.n: “Blute” has the old dative -e. “kehrte … zurück” (zurückkehren, to return) is separable. “frisch und gesund” = fresh and sound, hale and hearty.

sentences.t: Er sprach zum König „deine Treue soll nicht unbelohnt bleiben,“ und nahm die Häupter der Kinder, setzte sie auf, und bestrich die Wunde mit ihrem Blut, davon wurden sie im Augenblick wieder heil, sprangen herum und spielten fort, als wär ihnen nichts geschehen.
sentences.l: He spoke to the king, “your faithfulness shall not remain unrewarded,” and took the heads of the children, set them on, and smeared the wound with their blood; from that they became whole again in a moment, sprang about and went on playing, as if nothing had happened to them.
sentences.i: He said to the king, “Your faithfulness shall not go unrewarded,” and took the children’s heads, set them on, and smeared the wound with their blood; and at that they were made whole again in a moment, sprang about, and went on playing, as if nothing had happened to them.
sentences.n: “unbelohnt” = unrewarded. “Häupter” = heads (elevated plural of Haupt). “setzte … auf” (aufsetzen, to set on) and “spielten fort” (fortspielen, to go on playing) are separable. “heil” = whole, healed. The children are restored — the wound of the slaughter undone.

sentences.t: Nun war der König voll Freude, und als er die Königin kommen sah, versteckte er den getreuen Johannes und die beiden Kinder in einen großen Schrank.
sentences.l: Now the king was full of joy, and when he saw the queen coming, he hid faithful John and the two children in a great cupboard.
sentences.i: Now the king was full of joy, and when he saw the queen coming, he hid faithful John and the two children in a great cupboard.
sentences.n: “versteckte” (verstecken, to hide). He conceals them to test the queen’s heart before revealing the miracle.

sentences.t: Wie sie hereintrat, sprach er zu ihr „hast du gebetet in der Kirche?“
sentences.l: As she stepped in, he spoke to her, “have you prayed in church?”
sentences.i: As she came in, he said to her, “Have you prayed in church?”
sentences.n: “gebetet” = prayed (beten). “hereintrat” (hereintreten, to step in) is separable.

sentences.t: „Ja,“ antwortete sie, „aber ich habe beständig an den treuen Johannes gedacht, daß er so unglücklich durch uns geworden ist.“
sentences.l: “Yes,” she answered, “but I have constantly thought of faithful John, that he became so unfortunate through us.”
sentences.i: “Yes,” she answered, “but I kept thinking of faithful John, how wretched he became through us.”
sentences.n: “beständig” = constantly. “an … denken” = to think of (denken + an); “gedacht” is the participle. “unglücklich” = unfortunate, wretched.

sentences.t: Da sprach er „liebe Frau, wir können ihm das Leben wieder geben, aber es kostet uns unsere beiden Söhnlein, die müssen wir opfern.“
sentences.l: Then he spoke, “dear wife, we can give him his life again, but it costs us our two little sons; those we must sacrifice.”
sentences.i: Then he said, “Dear wife, we can give him back his life, but it will cost us our two little sons — those we must sacrifice.”
sentences.n: “opfern” = to sacrifice. The king puts the same terrible choice to the queen that the stone put to him.

sentences.t: Die Königin ward bleich und erschrack im Herzen, doch sprach sie „wir sinds ihm schuldig wegen seiner großen Treue.“
sentences.l: The queen grew pale and took fright in her heart, yet she spoke, “we owe it to him because of his great faithfulness.”
sentences.i: The queen turned pale and was stricken at heart, yet she said, “We owe it to him, for his great faithfulness.”
sentences.n: “ward bleich” = grew pale (ward, archaic preterite of werden). “sinds” = sind es. “jemandem etwas schuldig sein” = to owe someone something. “wegen” (+ genitive) = because of.

sentences.t: Da freute er sich daß sie dachte wie er gedacht hatte, gieng hin und schloß den Schrank auf, holte die Kinder und den treuen Johannes heraus und sprach „Gott sei gelobt, er ist erlöst, und unsere Söhnlein haben wir auch wieder,“ und erzählte ihr wie sich alles zugetragen hatte.
sentences.l: Then he rejoiced that she thought as he had thought, went over and unlocked the cupboard, fetched out the children and faithful John and spoke, “God be praised, he is redeemed, and our little sons we have again too,” and told her how everything had come about.
sentences.i: Then he rejoiced that she thought as he had thought, went over and unlocked the cupboard, brought out the children and faithful John, and said, “God be praised — he is delivered, and we have our little sons again too,” and told her how everything had come about.
sentences.n: “gieng hin” (hingehen), “schloß … auf” (aufschließen, to unlock), “holte … heraus” (herausholen) are separable. “Gott sei gelobt” = God be praised (gelobt, from loben). “erlöst” = redeemed, delivered. “sich zutragen” = to come about.

sentences.t: Da lebten sie zusammen in Glückseligkeit bis an ihr Ende.
sentences.l: Then they lived together in bliss until their end.
sentences.i: And so they lived together in bliss until their end.
sentences.n: “Glückseligkeit” = bliss, felicity — a solemn closing formula, ‘happily ever after until their end.’
